| Lamb and Mutton | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Category | Meat and poultry |

Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients  | Basic foodstuffs | Meat and poultry

**Lamb** and **mutton** refer to the meat from sheep,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2) with terminology largely depending on age; meat from younger sheep is lamb,[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2) while meat from older sheep is referred to as mutton.

## Production

[edit | edit source]

Sheep intended for meat may be grass- or grain-fed. In some regions of production, the meat may be aged after slaughter.[[3]](#cite_note-:5-3)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

### Age

[edit | edit source]

The exact terminology used to describe lamb and mutton varies according to culture and regional regulation. There is no single consensus age at which lamb becomes mutton.[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2) One guideline suggests that it becomes mutton when the sheep grows its first permanent teeth;[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2) others define lamb as younger than 1 year old and mutton as older than 1 year.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)

A number of specialized terms exist to refer to the age of the sheep.

* **Spring lamb:** older term that used to connote young lamb but doesn't have as much meaning nowadays[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)
* **Sucking/suckling lamb:** young lamb fed only milk, not grass or grain;[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) very pale, tender, and mild[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)
* **Milk lamb:** 30–40 days[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)
* **Baby lamb:** 6–10 weeks[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)
* **Agneau blanc/laiton:** 70–150 days old[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)
* **Young lamb:** 0–6 months[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)
* **Broutard:** 6–9 months[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)
* **Older lamb:** 6–12 months[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)
* **Yearling mutton:** 12–16 months[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)
* **Mutton:** ≥16 months;[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) strong flavor and texture[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)

### Grading

[edit | edit source]

After slaughter, the entire carcass is graded according to both age and marbling of the meat.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) Younger meat with higher marbling of the lean tissue gets a higher grade.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) The younger the animal, the less marbling is required for a high grade, and an older animal would require more marbling to achieve the same grade as a younger one.[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)

### Flavor

[edit | edit source]

Overall, lamb and mutton have an earthier, gamier flavor than something like beef.**[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)** The flavor of the meat depends on a combination of breed, age, and food source. Often, specialized wool breeds and older animals have a stronger flavor.[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)**[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)** Grain- and milk-fed lamb tends to be milder than grass-fed,[[3]](#cite_note-:5-3)**[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)** all other variables being equal. Animals reared grazing on salt marshland tend to have a specialized flavor and are called **pré-salé**.[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

### Texture

[edit | edit source]

Like with other meats, the younger the animal the more tender the meat.[[9]](#cite_note-:9-9) Overall, lamb and mutton have a finer grain than does beef,[[3]](#cite_note-:5-3) and lamb is generally considered a fatty meat.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) Note, however, that lamb fat is generally not considered very appetizing.[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)

## Cuts

[edit | edit source]

The main primal cuts of lamb from front to back are head, shoulder/chuck, rack/rib, breast, loin, and leg;[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)**[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)**[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) these are then often broken down into smaller cuts.[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) Because the animal is smaller, it is not always divided in half by splitting the backbone[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)—these pairs are called saddles.[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) Many cuts take the form of roasts and chops.

| Primal cut | Definition | Flavor/Texture | Description | Use | Subprimal cut | Subprimal description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Loin | Located between the ribs and the hip[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Very tender and lean[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Can be whole as a saddle or or split down the backbone into two;[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) can be bone-in or deboned[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) | Dry-heat cooking until rare or medium-rare;[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) good for kebabs[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Loin roast | Entire loin, shaped into a roast; often rolled and tied;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) wrapping with the belly flap protects the meat when cooking.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) |
| Loin chops | Loin eye and tenderloin muscles;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) when cut from a paired saddle you get double loin chops with a butterfly appearance;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) can be bone-in or boneless;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) the latter can be cut to any thickness;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) akin to T-bone beef steaks.[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) |
| Loin crosscut/British loin chop | Saddled pair of loin chops with bone, attached to a flap of belly meat[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) |
| Tenderloin/loin eye | Long muscle running along spine;[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) most tender part of the animal; Small and uncommon as a single cut; usually left in other cuts but can be cut into medallions.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) |
| Shoulder | Front part of the animal, including first few ribs, shoulder blade, arm[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)**[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)**[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Relatively tough; strongly flavored and fatty**[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)**[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) | Very large cut;[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) contains a complicated bone and muscular structure;[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) difficult to fabricate;[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) square-cut shoulder has foreshank removed;**[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)** can be whole and bone-in, boneless, or broken down into chops, stew meat, and ground meat;[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) generally a less expensive cut of lamb.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) | Does best with lower, slower, and moister heat cooking methods due to the tougher tissue[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Shoulder chop | Does well when brined;[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) cook to medium-rare and let rest before serving;[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) blade chop is from the top of the blade and is slightly more tender than arm chop;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) arm chop is from lower part of front leg;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) boneless shoulder chops are easier to eat.[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) |
| Shoulder roast | Cut from back of shoulder;[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) often deboned and rolled into a roast, but can be bone-in;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) can be stuffed;[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) smaller alternative option to the leg.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) |
| Leg/Hindshank | Rear leg of the animal[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Tender to slightly more tough as you move from the top (sirloin) end to the bottom (shank) end.[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Contains several different bones;[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) can be whole, halved, deboned, butterflied, stuffed, cut into skewer cubes, etc.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Variety of cooking styles depending on size and tenderness (roasting, braising, etc);[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) bone-in, large cuts are harder to cook evenly through.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) | Leg chops/steaks | More tender the closer they are to the top/sirloin;[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) can have the femur cross-section[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) |
| Steamship | Entire leg with the shank end frenched and minus the pelvic bone.[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) |
| Top round | Smaller roast cut from the top of the leg[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) |
| Shank | Shin region of the leg;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) consists of tough lean meat and connective tissue;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) may be cut into cross-sections;[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) often braised to tenderize it.[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) |
| Sirloin | Top cut of the leg; bone-in or boneless; does well roasted[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) |
| Breast/brisket | Region next to the shoulder, below the rack, and in front of the belly.**[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)**[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Fairly fatty cut[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Fairly small cut[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Can be stuffed, roasted, barbecued, or braised[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Denver ribs | Breast minus plate, rib tips, and cartilage;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) small section of ribs with a good amount of meat between them;[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) sometimes called spare ribs and can sub for pork ribs.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) |
| Riblets | Very small amount of meat, which is tough and fatty.[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) |
| Rack/ribs | Main rib portion of the animal; between the shoulder and loin;[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) contains 7–8 total ribs[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Tender, delicate flavor[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | May be frenched (bones trimmed and scraped);[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) thick fat cap keeps meat moist[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4); usually left whole as a roast or or cut into rib chops.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) | Suited for dry-heat and medium-rare cooking.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) | Rib chops | Thin layer of fat; quick-cooking;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) very expensive;[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) best when quickly cooked by grilling, broiling, or searing.[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) |
| Crown rib roast | Two racks intersecting and sewn together in a circular crown shape;[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2) should be roasted.[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) |
| Neck |  | Tough and meaty[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) |  | Should be braised or stewed for tenderness[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) |  |  |
| Foreshank |  | Meaty and hearty[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) | May be cut osso-bucco style[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) | Braising[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) |  |  |
| Head |  |  |  | Roasting, braising, depending on the cut;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) in charcuterie[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) | Tongue | Dense and tough, best braised to develop tenderness.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) |
| Cheeks | Very fibrous and good braised.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) |

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

When selecting lamb and mutton, use the same basic criteria as for other varieties of meat. You're also looking for meat that has a fine texture and red color,[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) without yellowed fat.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) Choose the appropriate age and cut for your desired application. Like other fresh meats, keep in the fridge for a few days or in the freezer for a few months.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Because lamb and mutton tend to have a strong flavor and fatty texture, they are often paired with strong flavors like seasonings and acids.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) It plays a significant role in the cuisines of North Africa, the Middle East, and Central Asia.[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)**[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)**
